title: A Veteran Before the Law School
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in June 2011 at the federal courthouse that bears his name in Columbia, South Carolina, Judge Matthew J. Perry Jr. asks to begin before law school, recounting that he was born in Columbia in 1921, was a South Carolina State College student when Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, received his draft notice, and reported for duty in January 1943 to a segregated Quartermaster Corps that trained at Camp Van Dorn, Mississippi, having grown up accustomed to the racial segregation imposed by law.

title: A Window Outside the Kitchen
summary: Perry describes the awakening that began as he grew intellectually and questioned segregation's justification, centering on a weekend pass home by train at the end of basic training, when he changed trains in Birmingham, Alabama, and during a two-hour layover in damp weather could not enter the terminal restaurant, having to order a sandwich through a window outside the kitchen alongside other Black soldiers and passengers.

title: They Served the Italian Prisoners
summary: He recounts looking into that restaurant and seeing Italian prisoners of war, enemy combatants in uniform, being served courteously by young waitresses while he, an American soldier in his country's uniform fresh from basic training, was denied the privilege, a contrast that turned familiar indignity into a feeling of insult and left him wondering what could be done, a memory that stayed with him as he served in England, France, Belgium, and Germany before his discharge in January 1946.

title: Two Cases Tried Back to Back
summary: Returning to Columbia to finish his undergraduate work at South Carolina State, Perry tells how he and fellow veterans went to the federal court on Laurel Street as spectators and watched two cases tried back to back, the first brought by George Elmore against South Carolina election officials challenging the white primaries that denied Black citizens the vote, argued by Thurgood Marshall, whom Perry had never seen before, and Robert L. Carter, building on the Smith v. Allwright precedent he only later understood.

title: A Name We Found Distasteful
summary: Perry describes the second case, brought by John H. Wrighten, a young Black American suing for entry to the University of South Carolina's law school he and other Black students could not attend, and notes that South Carolina State College, the only state-supported Black institution, was named in statute the colored normal agricultural and mechanical college at Orangeburg, a name students found distasteful and popularly replaced.

title: What Will My Life's Work Be
summary: Impressed by the lawyers' performance and contemplating his life's work as he developed into manhood, Perry recounts weighing business and medicine before deciding against them, drawn instead toward a field that might equip him to understand the reasoning behind the racial practices he had become aware of and to help find solutions, settling privately on the study of law at Howard University where the lawyers he admired had trained.

title: A Jim Crow Dump
summary: Perry recounts Judge J. Waties Waring deciding for Elmore in the voting case and for Wrighten in the school case, ordering South Carolina either to admit Wrighten to the university law school, provide a separate but substantially equal one, or cease offering legal education, and how the state opened the very inadequate law school at South Carolina State that Thurgood Marshall called a Jim Crow dump, hiring a dean and teachers while Perry finished his undergraduate studies.

title: You Better Understand What You Face
summary: Perry tells how the new law school needed students and word spread of a veteran named Perry who claimed he would study law, prompting someone to urge him to sit in and understand what he would face at Howard, and how, having only part-time courses, he monitored classes and ended up remaining at the institution, where well-trained teachers from Harvard, Columbia, Kansas, and Iowa instilled a concept of hard work.

title: Blame Me for the Bar Exam
summary: Perry explains South Carolina's diploma privilege, by which law graduates were admitted without a bar examination, and how officials creating the South Carolina State school persuaded the legislature to require the examination of all graduates, so that his 1951 class was the first to sit it while the prior class of 1950 was exempt, and he, the only one of his group to pass, jokes that anyone who has taken the bar since 1951 can blame him.

title: Rubbernecking Into Briggs v. Elliott
summary: Having just graduated and taken the bar with results still unpublished, Perry travels with a few others to Charleston for the well-publicized Briggs v. Elliott trial, a major challenge to segregated education, and recalls hordes of people and how, being tall with a long neck, he rubbernecked over the crowd and squeezed into the courtroom to witness the trial, then turns to the difficulty of earning a living as one of the few Black lawyers in South Carolina.

title: A One-Room Office and an Underwood
summary: Unable to find any job or law clerk position, Perry explains that opening his own office became inevitable, and how on his mother and a successful funeral-home cousin's advice he chose Spartanburg, which had no Black attorneys while Columbia with about three was considered crowded, setting up a one-room secondhand office with an old Underwood typewriter and typing his own documents, struggling to attract business as a young inexperienced Black lawyer until his cousin's bond signing brought a first traffic case to municipal court.

title: A Notice of Appeal in the Briefcase
summary: Perry recounts his first foray into the municipal court of a judge he calls a racist of the first order, who let young white lawyers' clients off with small fines but found Perry's client guilty without hearing evidence and imposed the maximum thirty days or a hundred dollars, a crushing result for his reputation, and how he learned to draft a notice of appeal within the five-day window on grounds the decision was contrary to the evidence, so that on a later case he pulled the notice from his still-new briefcase, asked the shocked judge to accept service, told him it had been his decision every time, and won a reversal before the circuit judge.

title: Day Number Six in Union County
summary: Perry tells how word spread and calls came from adjoining counties, including the Union County Voters League about a young illiterate man given a gargantuan sentence by a magistrate, who reached Perry only on the sixth day after the five-day appeal window had expired, leading him to file a petition for a writ of habeas corpus for the jailed man whose family had no resources, and to win his first victory in the South Carolina Supreme Court.

title: A Cooperating Attorney
summary: Perry explains becoming associated with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund as a cooperating attorney, an unpaid role that drew a small stipend per supported case, and separately with the NAACP Office of General Counsel, two distinct organizations whose division the public did not know, attending seminars and national conventions where lawyers called in from around the country formed a network of associates, friendships, and shared knowledge of what was happening in various places.

title: Moot Court at Howard
summary: Perry recalls traveling to Washington with South Carolina colleagues when the cases were set for Supreme Court argument and how they were first argued moot-court style at Howard University Law School, where professors and distinguished lawyers mimicked the sitting justices and their judicial philosophies, an exhilarating dry run featuring Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Spottswood W. Robinson III, and Jack Greenberg, with Constance Baker Motley then a neophyte on staff who argued neither case.

title: To the Back of the Bus
summary: Perry turns to Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company, describing Columbia's segregated bus service that required Black riders to the rear under state law, and the case of Sarah Mae Fleming, a young domestic who sat near the dividing line and, ordered by the driver to move and barred from the front door, was shoved and claimed she was struck in the abdomen, after which she made her situation known to local people.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company

title: The First One on the Record
summary: Perry recounts Modjeska Monteith Simkins sending Fleming to white attorney Phil Wittenberg, who filed suit, incurred the white community's wrath including a cross burned in his yard, and got out of the case after the Fourth Circuit reversed a dismissal, after which Perry joined Lincoln Jenkins Jr. at the trial level and lost before an unfriendly judge though the precedent stood, concluding that Fleming's case, set in motion June 22, 1954, was the first bus case on the record, preceding Browder v. Gayle and Rosa Parks in Montgomery, before the interview closes in the courthouse named for him.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company
